text: I usually have something simple like toast with butter and jam, maybe some scrambled eggs if I'm feeling peckish before heading out for my morning walk. Or sometimes I'll make myself an omelette with vegetables from our little garden. Nothing too fancy, just a quiet start to the day. Would love a cuppa tea while I eat, though!

I usually have a simple sandwich or leftovers from last night's dinner, accompanied by some fruit and perhaps a cup of tea. If I'm feeling peckish, I might treat myself to something special like scones and jam with a pot of tea, which is always lovely. But generally, my lunches tend to be fairly understated and easy to prepare – just fuel for getting me through the rest of the morning before my afternoon slump sets in!
I'm usually in bed by 10 o'clock. My body seems to have a bit of a rhythm going, and I find I feel rested best after getting some sleep before 11 pm.

I think a lovely cuppa is essential first thing in the morning, isn't it? It gives me a chance to sit quietly, collect my thoughts before starting the day. After that, getting some fresh air and taking a short walk around the block helps clear my head and sets me up for the day ahead. A bit of exercise never hurts either – usually some gentle stretching or yoga to loosen up those stiff joints! In the evenings, I love unwinding with a good book or a nice warm bath. And of course, spending time with my grandkids always puts a smile on my face. They're such bright lights in my life, even if they can be a handful sometimes! All in all, simple pleasures like these make up my daily self-care routine, keeping me feeling grounded and content.

I try to be friendly and approachable, making an effort to chat with colleagues during breaks or while we're all waiting for meetings. It helps build relationships and gets us talking about common topics outside work. At our school, there was a regular tea break where everyone gathered – it really brought people together. Since retiring, my son occasionally takes me out for cups of coffee or we meet fellow retirees who share similar hobbies; those informal gatherings have helped me stay updated on what's happening locally. When needed, I'll attend staff meetings and contribute my thoughts too.

I find a regular daily routine helps me stay focused while working from home. I try to establish an 8-to-noon workday, which works quite well for my chronotype. During this period, I like to have some fresh air and take breaks every hour or so to stretch and move around – perhaps taking a walk outside or tidying up a bit. It's essential for me to set aside dedicated workspace time, free from distractions such as television and personal chores. My desk is near a window where I can watch birds and plants growing; it really boosts morale! A gentle reminder to myself that there's still nature out there even though I'm indoors. Plus, having a cuppa nearby always does wonders for concentration.

I'm getting more comfortable with them now, thanks to my grandsons helping me set everything up. They're always checking in on each other and keeping an eye out for me, so I have someone to rely on for tech support. We usually stick to simple chats like catching up on how everyone's doing – we talk about school, work, or what's going on in our lives. It's lovely being able to feel close even though we live far apart. Occasionally, we'll watch a film together or play games while talking; those moments make it all worthwhile.

I find that having a sense of purpose helps me stay motivated. As someone who enjoys being busy and productive, I make sure my daily routine is still filled with meaningful activities like tending to my garden, reading, or spending time with loved ones. It might seem simple, but knowing what needs attending to each morning gives me direction and allows me to tackle tasks one by one, which really helps. Plus, keeping myself accountable – whether it's chatting with a neighbour, meeting a friend for tea, or simply setting reminders on my phone – does wonders for staying focused and avoiding procrastination!

I think I'm quite flexible with adapting to changes at work, although I must admit it took me a while to adjust to certain aspects of modern workflows when I first started out. As an experienced admin professional, I've had my fair share of juggling multiple tasks and adjusting to shifting priorities. When something comes along that requires re-prioritization, I try to assess what really needs attention and focus on those areas first. It might involve delegating some tasks where possible, communicating clearly with colleagues and supervisors, and being open to finding solutions together. For instance, during my tenure as school secretary, we'd have to adapt our schedules to accommodate staffing shortages due to illness or absences; having regular meetings to discuss priorities helped us navigate these challenging periods more effectively. Overall, staying proactive, remaining communicative, and focusing on the essential objectives helps me manage changing priorities efficiently.

I think it's essential to be clear and concise while still being polite and considerate towards others. When there's an issue or a concern, I try to explain my thoughts and feelings directly, rather than beating around the bush. If necessary, I'll politely point out specific examples or facts to support my argument. At the same time, I'm always willing to listen to other people's viewpoints and take into account their perspective, even if we differ. It's all about finding common ground and making decisions that suit everyone involved. My manager has said I have a tendency to "get straight to the heart of the matter," which is something I strive for – getting to the essence of what really needs addressing without unnecessary fuss.

I think when I feel like I'm being misunderstood, my first instinct is to try and have a rational conversation about what's going on. Sometimes, people jump to conclusions because we only hear one side of the story, so I'll explain myself slowly and clearly, hoping to clarify any misconceptions. If needed, I might ask someone to tell me more about why they're misunderstanding me, which helps me understand where they're coming from too. In some cases, taking a step back and giving them space may be necessary – sometimes all parties need time to process information before discussing it further. But generally speaking, I believe open communication and honest dialogue can resolve misunderstandings quite effectively.

I love spending time with my grandkids! They always bring so much joy to our little family get-togethers. We usually spend a lot of time playing games together, like Monopoly or cards, or having competitions to see who can eat the most delicious homemade treats I bake for them. My grandson is quite the tech whiz now, and he loves showing me all the latest gadgets he's into. It's lovely seeing him develop his passions. We also make sure to have plenty of conversations about everything from school and sports to their favorite books and music. I adore listening to their thoughts and sharing some of my own stories from when I was younger. Family history is very dear to me, so we often sit around talking about where we come from and how our ancestors lived. Those moments really warm my heart. Of course, there's always laughter involved – whether it's teasing each other playfully or making silly faces while watching cartoons. And I'm happy to report that despite being an avid gardener myself, none of my grandkids seem interested in taking over my green thumb... yet! Maybe one day though! It truly feels wonderful to be surrounded by such loving company and feel grateful for every moment spent with these precious young people growing up before my eyes.

Honestly, I'm not too keen on those sorts of platforms. As a retired person, my focus has shifted towards more personal connections rather than professional networking. My son does try to get me involved occasionally, but I must admit I find all these new-fangled technologies quite overwhelming. If someone wants to make contact, an email or even a proper letter is fine with me. But as for joining some sort of "online profile" where people share every little thing...no thank you! It seems like so much fuss and bother. Give me a cuppa and a chat anytime, though.

I think I'd always try to be honest and open, but tactful too. You know, straight from the heart, but saying it gently so it doesn't cause unnecessary upset. Sometimes, it helps me take a deep breath before speaking, making sure my thoughts are ordered in my mind first. If needed, I'll choose words carefully, avoiding blame or being confrontational – focusing more on how I feel and what I need rather than attacking the other person. Of course, everyone is different, but generally, I believe an empathetic ear and genuine concern usually go a long way in having constructive conversations.

I'd say I'm quite straightforward, so I tend to speak my mind, even if others might think me a bit too blunt at times. If someone asks something I genuinely believe is wrong or unnecessary, I'll tell them why I feel that way, even if we're all having a cuppa together. It helps prevent people getting taken advantage of, if you ask me. Of course, being kind and polite still comes first – I wouldn't be mean-spirited; I simply value honesty above all else.

To be honest, I find them a bit intrusive. As someone who values my personal space and likes avoiding unnecessary clutter, I try to limit my engagement with those ads that seem too pushy. If something catches my eye or seems relevant, I might take note of it, but most of the time, I'll simply ignore them and switch channels or close the browser tab. It's all about being mindful of what's going into our lives, isn't it?

To be honest, I struggle a bit with keeping track of all those little windows open. Sometimes my grandchild will show me how to close them efficiently, but generally, I find myself clicking the red X one too many times! My brain isn't what it used to be, and multitasking is quite challenging these days. If I really need multiple pages going at once, like looking up information for our book club meeting, I'll usually stick to having only two or three open – the main page, any reference material, and maybe another tab for notes. But more often than not, I end up closing everything except for whatever I'm actively using. It might seem simple to some people, but trust me, it takes effort to remember where everything is!

I'm afraid I don't have much time for trolls or people who think it's okay to be nasty. When someone makes a comment like that, my first thought is usually "well, bless them" because I genuinely believe there must be something bothering them that's led them to behave like that. But then again, some people say those sorts of things regardless, so sometimes it takes me aback. If I feel strongly enough about what was said, I might politely point out how it came across as hurtful or unhelpful. My intention isn't always to argue, though – more often I try to steer conversations towards topics we all agree on or redirect the conversation to find common ground. I value constructive dialogue where everyone feels heard and respected. It really depends on the situation, I suppose. If I feel uncomfortable or threatened, I'll distance myself accordingly. But generally speaking, I'd rather focus on kindness and positivity. It seems the world could do with a bit more of that!

I'm afraid I don't know how to troubleshoot those sorts of issues myself! My grandsons are always happy to lend a hand. They're very knowledgeable about these new-fangled gadgets and usually sort me out in no time. If all else fails, we try contacting our son who's quite handy with computers too. He'll probably come round and fix everything for us. Or sometimes we have to wait until someone comes along and fixes them properly – much like my husband used to do before... Ah, he was brilliant with machines.

I like to think being organised is key. Every month, I sit down with my calendar and make a list of all the bills due soonest first – bank statements, council tax, utility bills... You know how it works. Then I check our joint account balance so we're sure we have enough money set aside before making payments. Once everything's sorted out, I transfer the necessary amounts into each individual accounts, double-checking again before hitting send. My late husband used to say it was a ritual almost more tedious than enjoyable, but he always knew we'd be alright afterwards! Now I carry on his tradition because consistency has been essential during retirement; can't afford any surprises, wot?!

I try to be quite mindful about my spending, even though I'm now semi-retired and have a bit more flexibility. It's hard sometimes because there are so many useful services out there, aren't there? But I ask myself, "Do I really need it?" And if the answer is no, then I consider cancelling. For instance, we had one of those meal kit subscription boxes. Sounds lovely, I thought, until it started costing us an arm and leg every month! So, I cancelled that one straight away. Now we stick to buying our groceries like normal people do. Another thing I look into is price increases – if something has gone up too much since I signed up, it might make sense to reconsider. We're always keeping an eye on what's coming due and cutting any recurring payments where costs seem excessive. Also helps me avoid getting caught out by hidden fees and charges. Of course, there are some subscriptions that genuinely bring joy – newspapers, streaming services... those sorts of things I'll probably never cut ties with, unless absolutely necessary. The key is being aware of how much money these expenses add up over time, and making sure it fits with our overall priorities. Simple principle, really.

Well, I think I'm a very frugal person, even now that I'm retired. Before my husband passed away, we'd always had to be mindful of our finances, so habits stuck! When it comes to making large decisions like replacing an appliance or buying something major, I try to weigh all my options really thoroughly, considering what will last longest and whether it'll fit into our budget. We've never been ones to go out and buy on impulse, and I still stick to my "waste not, want not" philosophy – every penny counts, isn't it? I usually spend some time researching and comparing prices, looking for deals or discounts wherever I can find them. And then, once I feel pretty sure I know exactly what I need, I make a list of priorities and allocate a specific amount I'm happy to pay. Once that's decided upon, I start saving specifically for it until I have enough money set aside, which might take longer for bigger items. It's almost become second nature to me, actually – I remember being quite diligent during my working days too! Now that I've got more free time, I'm grateful to apply those savings habits to whatever purchase is coming along. My grandkids sometimes tease me because I love getting bargains

I think I'm a bit of an old-fashioned person when it comes to purchasing decisions like that. When faced with the choice between something new and something pre-owned, I usually weigh the pros and cons rather than jumping straight into either option. For instance, if what we're talking about is something essential – say, a washing machine – then I'd consider whether there's been any significant improvements since my current one was last replaced, how much wear and tear mine has got on it, and whether it still does its job adequately before deciding whether to splash out on brand-new or try to find a decent deal on someone else's hand-me-down. That being said, for more discretionary items such as books or even perhaps some furniture, I tend to lean towards buying second-hand whenever possible – mainly because it helps stretch our budgets a little better, reduces waste, and gives me peace of mind knowing exactly where everything came from! Still, if I wouldn’t be getting fantastic value, I might opt for newer stuff instead; ultimately, depends really. What's your take on this sort of thing? Do people these days buy mostly new, all the way? Or have attitudes changed?

I've always believed in being frugal and avoiding unnecessary expenses, including debt. My husband used to say "a penny saved is a penny earned" all our lives together. Even before he passed away, we had instilled in each other the importance of living below one's means and managing finances wisely. We'd always tried to save something every month, whether it was putting aside a bit into a savings account or using what little free time to tend to my vegetable patch – there's value in watching your money grow organically! Nowadays, since becoming financially independent, I make sure to stick closely to my budgets. I'm very particular about tracking where my income goes, making sure nothing slips past me unnoticed. Sales, discounts, and coupons become an essential part of my daily routine; I know exactly how much I have available for spending and when. And let me tell you, having that sense of control has been incredibly liberating! It reminds me that true freedom isn't found in material possessions, but rather in peace of mind. Plus, it allows me to treat myself occasionally – like buying those nice books I love so dearly!

I make a point to check out those seasonal sales whenever there's something specific we need, like winter coats or Christmas presents for the grandkids. My daughter always says I'm quite clever with bargains! But generally speaking, I try to avoid impulse buying – I remember my mother used to say "a penny saved is a penny earned" so I stick to sticking to our budget. Occasionally, though, I might treat myself to something nice during an actual sale, provided I've been saving specifically for it.

I'm a bit old-fashioned, I suppose, but I believe in moderation and balance. When cooking for myself, I make sure to measure out ingredients and serve portions accordingly. My husband always said I was very good at making food go further! Now, since he's gone, I still try to be mindful of what I eat, even though my grandkids love coming over and eating all the leftovers! It's funny, I find that controlling portions isn't so much about depriving yourself, is it? It's more about enjoying your favourite foods while keeping an eye on how full you're feeling. A nice slice of cake or a small serving of cheese and crackers before dinner – these little pleasures make me happy, rather than devouring everything in sight. Plus, being prepared makes a big difference. Meal prep, like chopping vegetables ahead of time or having a stash of fresh fruit in the fridge...these simple habits have helped me develop healthier eating habits over the years. And of course, taking pleasure in cooking itself has been wonderful therapy during difficult periods – there's something soothing about creating nourishment for oneself, don't you think? Still, sometimes I allow myself to indulge every now and then; after all, life should be enjoyed, too! As long as I know

I think a gentle, empathetic approach is essential in therapy or counselling. You know, someone who listens attentively and allows me to express myself freely, without interrupting or judging too harshly. A bit like having a conversation with an old friend, really. It should be a safe space where I feel encouraged to share my thoughts and feelings, and where the therapist helps me uncover patterns or insights that might have been hiding beneath the surface. And, of course, it'd be nice if there were some homework or exercises to work on outside of sessions – sometimes it feels like talking therapies aren't much more effective unless we put what was discussed into action afterwards!

Preventing burnout is essential for me, especially now that I'm fully retired. You know, people say it's all downhill after retirement, but I think there's still so much value in staying mentally and physically active. For me, taking breaks throughout the day helps a lot – whether that's stepping outside for some fresh air, having a nice cuppa, or simply rearranging my priorities for the morning. I find my peak productivity hours are between 8 am and 12 pm, which suits me nicely because I have fewer distractions during those hours. I make sure to plan out what needs to be done each day before starting work, breaking tasks into smaller chunks whenever possible, and then leaving space for flexibility later in case something pops up. It sounds simple, but it makes such a difference! Of course, exercise has been crucial too. Even though I'm not as spry as I used to be, regular strolls around the block, combined with some gentle stretching exercises in the evenings, really does seem to boost my mood and reduce any fatigue. Oh, and spending time surrounded by nature always leaves me feeling refreshed! Whether it's tending to my little veggie patch or watching birds flitting about in our backyard – being outdoors feels like medicine for me

I think a combination of discipline and establishing a calming bedtime routine helps me improve my sleep quality. It starts with sticking to a regular sleep schedule – going to bed between 10 pm and waking up at 6:30 am every day, even on weekends. Then, before getting into bed, I try to relax with some gentle stretching exercises, perhaps listening to soothing music or nature sounds. A warm bath is another ritual I find comforting. And of course, making sure my bedroom is dark, cool, and quiet does wonders too! The key is being mindful of screens before bed; I avoid using electronic devices like phones or tablets until at least an hour before bedtime. Getting enough exercise during the day seems to be helpful as well. Nothing drastic changes here, really, just a bit more consistency, I suppose!

I try to mix it up a bit, really. On weekdays, I usually start my morning with some gentle stretching while having my tea, then take a brisk walk along the nearby park before breakfast. In the evenings, once I'm done with dinner, I might join one of our local WI groups – we have an excellent chair yoga session every Thursday afternoon which is lovely. Occasionally, my grandkids will come round and play hide-and-seek in the garden; it gets me moving! If the weather permits, I'll go out into the garden myself and tend to my vegetables or flowers. It helps keep me feeling grounded and relaxed. A little bit of fresh air never hurts either!

I think I tend to weigh the pros and cons quite thoroughly before making a decision. It's almost like having all my thoughts jotted down in my notebook, if you know what I mean! But seriously, I suppose it's because I'm someone who likes to consider multiple viewpoints and make sure everything is thought out properly before acting. It might sound old-fashioned, but I still believe in taking one step back, gathering information, weighing the advantages and disadvantages, and considering how each option will impact my daily life. Of course, sometimes you have to trust your instincts too – but I find that gives me peace of mind knowing I've done due diligence beforehand. Plus, I've found that being methodical helps prevent impulsive decisions which can be costly mistakes. My husband always used to say I was meticulous; perhaps he wasn't entirely wrong! Anyway, once I feel fairly satisfied that I've covered all angles, then I try to pick the best choice based on common sense and my priorities. And remember, I've learned that patience and curiosity really take you much further than rushing through life, so even though it may seem slow going, I reckon it's worth the extra effort in the end.

I think incorporating feedback is an essential part of any solution, whether personal or professional. For me, it's all about being open-minded and acknowledging where others have made a valid point. If someone tells me something hasn't worked out quite as planned, I try to listen actively and ask clarifying questions to make sure I fully grasp what didn't work so I can improve future attempts. Then, I take note of those insights and consider them when devising alternative approaches. Sometimes, re-evaluating my initial idea helps me come up with better options altogether. In some cases, taking constructive feedback might even lead me to completely alter course – which has happened more frequently than once! Ultimately, using people's input makes everyone involved feel heard and valued, which usually results in a far superior outcome in the end.

I think my approach to analytical thinking is quite methodical and step-by-step. When faced with a problem, I like to take my time, gather all relevant information, break it down into smaller parts, and then start piecing everything together. It helps me make sense of complex issues and identify patterns. I'm also someone who likes having a clear plan and sticking to it, which I suppose could be seen as part of being organised. However, sometimes people might say I can be too critical or perfectionistic – but I believe it's essential to have high standards in certain areas.

I find that writing things down helps me solidify my thoughts. If something is bothering me or unclear, I'll jot some notes, make a list, or even draw diagrams to break it down step-by-step. It really clarifies what I'm struggling with and allows me to identify patterns or connections I might have missed otherwise. Sometimes, talking through an issue with someone who knows me fairly well will also give me fresh insights – like having a cuppa with my son while we walk his dog! A bit of reflection and conversation usually does wonders for getting my bearings.

I think identifying areas for growth is quite a personal thing for me. As someone who tends to be fairly organised and methodical in my daily routines, I'd say I'm generally pretty aware of what works well for me and what might need some fine-tuning. However, there have been instances where I felt like I could improve my tech-savviness, perhaps because I sometimes struggle to update software or troubleshoot issues. My grandson has actually helped me out more than once! If I had to pinpoint one area, it would probably be getting more comfortable using newer technologies – it's all so overwhelming at first! But overall, I believe being open to feedback, whether from others or even myself, helps me spot those little cracks in my usual way of going about things. Maybe we're never too old to try something new, though; I mean, look how much I've adapted since retirement, eh? Nowadays, I tend to focus more on making sure everything runs smoothly in my daily routine – ensuring the grandkids visit regularly, keeping the garden looking tidy, and staying connected with friends. It's lovely to feel grounded and content, knowing these simple pleasures bring joy to my life.

Well, you know, I think it's all about being open to the idea of change, isn't it? And then, really considering why we're making a particular decision. What will be the benefits, what might go wrong, how can we mitigate those risks... that sort of thing. For me, having a bit of patience and taking my time helps too. I've found that sometimes rushing into something without giving it thought can lead to more trouble than necessary. But once I feel like I have a solid foundation, whether it's a new project at work or starting a new hobby outside of the home, I try to take small steps forward, gradually building momentum. And, of course, talking it out with people who trust my judgment always seems to help me make clearer decisions. As an old saying goes: "I've learned that patience and curiosity get you further than rushing through life ever did."

I cherish my reading time, actually! Since retiring, I have more flexibility with my daily routines. In the morning, before anyone wakes up, I like to sit comfortably here in my favourite armchair, make a cuppa tea, and lose myself in a good novel or biography. It's lovely to unwind and escape into another world for a bit. If I'm lucky, I might even squeeze in some reading during my lunch break, whether that's in the park or at the local café. And sometimes, I'll spend an hour or two on Sunday evenings before bed, catching up on any newspapers or magazines I missed throughout the week. My grandkids always tease me about how much I love getting lost in books – but what can I say? There's nothing quite like curling up with a good story!

I usually find out about new authors or genres through recommendations from people I trust, like my grandkids. They're always getting into different books and sharing them with me, which is lovely. Sometimes we have a chat afterwards about what we thought of it, and that helps spark interest in something else. Occasionally, I'll stumble upon an author while browsing in a charity shop or library sale – there's nothing quite like discovering someone completely unknown who writes beautifully! My late husband used to read history novels before he passed away; now his son reads those sorts of books instead, so sometimes we discuss our shared love of the subject matter and recommend each other titles. And of course, the local library does its part too – they send newsletters and display new releases from various publishers. It all combines together that way...

I usually tend to knit or crochet, actually. It helps me relax and unwind, especially during my daily bus rides into town. The rhythmic motion is quite soothing, and I find myself getting lost in patterns and colours. Occasionally, if I'm feeling more energetic, I might work on a jigsaw puzzle too – there's something calming about fitting pieces together, don't you think?

I'm quite fond of getting lost in a good story, whether it's an audio book or podcast! When my husband was alive, we'd listen together before bed, and now I find myself doing it more since he passed away. I like how it allows me to unwind and escape into another world while still being able to focus on something other than everyday worries. The main thing is, though, I need to be able to adjust the volume so I can hear what everyone else is saying – sometimes the sound gets too loud! But overall, I think they're wonderful for relaxation and expanding one's horizons.

I love staying active! You know, people say it's never too late to start something new, and I think that's true. For me, there's nothing like getting out into my garden in the morning, tending to my vegetables and flowers. It's a great way to connect with nature and feel productive all at once. There's something so satisfying about watching those little seeds sprout into beautiful blooms or juicy tomatoes ripen on the vine. And of course, I'm always eager to share some fresh produce with the grandkids! When I'm feeling more energetic, I might take a leisurely walk along our local park paths or visit one of the nearby gardens. Nothing beats strolling among the roses and taking in the sights and sounds – it clears my mind and gets me ready for the day ahead. Occasionally, I'll join forces with friends for a game of bowls or a spot of bridge; we have such a lovely time chatting while keeping an eye on each other's scores. But honestly, most of my exercise is still done at a snail's pace... whether it's watering my plants, dusting the furniture, or simply being mindful of my posture during meetings! All these little habits add up, I suppose! My motto has been "patience

I thoroughly enjoy spending time alone. As someone who values her personal space and is a morning person, I find solace in being able to start my day however I like, without any distractions. A cuppa, some reading, maybe a bit of gentle stretching – it sets me up nicely for tackling whatever the day may bring. It's lovely having the freedom to focus on myself, whether that's indulging in a favourite hobby like baking or simply enjoying a peaceful walk in our little garden. Time alone allows me to recharge and feel refreshed, which makes me more productive and present when I'm interacting with others later in the day. Plus, there's something satisfying about achieving everything I wanted done before taking a break, knowing I have plenty of time to unwind afterwards.

I love experimenting with new foods! When trying a new cuisine, I tend to start with something simple like pasta or stir-fry dishes where I can adjust the seasoning to my taste. If someone offers me a dish with unfamiliar ingredients, I'll ask them what each component is before taking a bite so I know exactly what I'm eating. Sometimes, I might stick close to traditional methods while others prefer a more modern take – either way, I appreciate the effort that goes into preparing those meals.

I find that mixing different types of activities really helps me avoid getting bored. You know, people might think an older lady like myself should be content watching telly all day, but I'm actually quite keen on keeping my mind and body active! For instance, every Saturday morning is reserved for a spot of gardening – nothing too strenuous, just pottering about among the vegetables and whatnot. And then there's my book club, which meets once a month; we discuss some cracking novel, usually something historical or classic literature. It's lovely catching up with those chaps and sharing our thoughts. In between, I have my little hobby sessions where I work on crafting bits and pieces for the grandkids – making them something special always brings a smile to their faces, even if mine does wrinkle a bit more than usual! If the weather permits, I love taking short walks along the nearby canal towpath. Fresh air never hurt anyone, eh? It's funny how these simple pleasures add so much joy to one's daily life. What about you, dear? How do you manage to mix things up during your free time?

I'm a bit old-fashioned, I suppose. For my main hobbies like gardening and baking, I tend to jot down notes in a little notebook. It helps me remember what worked out all right and what didn't so I can adjust for next time. I find writing everything down really helpful, even though I know some people might say I should be using a digital calendar instead. To be honest, I'm still getting used to these new-fangled devices – my grandkids are always telling me I need one! But I prefer pen and paper; there's something satisfying about putting pen to paper. Occasionally, I'll make a note on my tablet because someone is giving me instructions or showing me how to do something new, but overall, notebooks remain my go-to way of keeping track of those sorts of things.

I find organisation is key to keeping my life running smoothly! For me, it all starts with a daily planner – I have two main ones actually, one for work-related stuff like meetings and deadlines, and another for personal reminders and habits like taking medication and watering plants. It's become second nature now, really. In terms of paperwork, I'm quite diligent about filing away documents and receipts in labelled folders so nothing gets lost. My husband used to tease me about being a bit of an obsessive folder-maker, but he still has them even though we're widowed now! Nowadays, digital tools have made things easier too. We've got our calendars synced across devices, and I use notes app on my tablet to jot down quick thoughts or ideas before I forget them. I must admit, getting accustomed to those newer gadgets wasn't easy; took some trial-and-error figuring out how everything worked together. Still, once mastered, they save me loads of time. It's funny, people always ask me what my secrets are – honestly, most of it comes down to habit and knowing myself pretty well. Knowing which routines will make a difference and sticking to them helps prevent chaos building up. Simple principles, perhaps, but works for me, anyway!

Before leaving home, I make myself a cup of tea, put on my favourite coat, grab my handbag, and take my phone and keys out of the drawer. Sometimes, I'll stop by the kitchen window to check on the birds outside our little bird feeder – those sparrows always seem so busy flitting about! If I'm feeling extra tidy, I might even water the plants before heading out. It's become quite the ritual, getting ready for whatever the day brings. Now, shall we have another chat?

I try my best to be mindful of wasting energy while still keeping warm enough in our little house. We have double glazing, which has made a big difference already. I make sure to turn off lights, radiators, and electronics when not needed, and we only heat one room at a time rather than having central heating all the time. In winter, I wrap up warmly before going out, so there's no need to run the heating unnecessarily. When it does get really cold, I switch between using electric blankets and hot water bottles – simple but effective! My late husband always said he'd never go back to those dreadful gas bills, and now I'm carrying on his legacy, I suppose!

I must admit, I'm a bit bewildered by them all. To be honest, my son has been pushing me to get more modernised, but I find myself getting frustrated with how complicated everything is. He says he'll explain it again and again until I get it, which isn't exactly reassuring! Still, we have some basics like a voice-controlled radio and an Amazon Echo in the living room – mainly so our granddaughter can call us while playing games or watching something remotely. But overall, I'd say I prefer simplicity and stick to what I know works. Too much fussiness makes me feel uncomfortable. Oh dear, sometimes I wish I could turn the lights on without having to talk to the device first...

I think prioritising is quite simple really. You have your big picture goals – like staying healthy and independent, spending quality time with my grandkids – then there are smaller ones like keeping the garden tidy and making sure everything runs smoothly at home. It's all about weighing what's most important in any given moment. For instance, last weekend was a perfect example of that; we had our lovely granddaughter coming to visit and I wanted to make sure her room was spotless so she'd feel welcome. At the same time, I didn't want to be too fussed because I knew she'd still appreciate me having some downtime while I'm here. So, I managed to strike a balance between being present for her and taking care of myself. And that's how I approach it generally – assessing each situation and deciding where my priorities lie.
